title: Contrasting Effects of Functionalization in Binary and Medium-Entropy MXene Coatings for Corrosion Protection
authors: Aqsa Fayyaz, Ulises Martin Diaz, Jianyu Dai, Homero Castaneda, Chenglin Wu
abstract: Developing scalable and environmentally benign anticorrosion coatings is critical for protecting steel infrastructure in chloride-rich environments. Here, a nacre-inspired multilayer epoxy coating reinforced with four MXene systems is investigated. This architecture forms a dense lamellar network that increases diffusion tortuosity and introduces electroactive surfaces for ion interactions. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) confirms that the multilayer design increases coating resistance from ~103 to ~108 Ohm/cm2. A clear performance hierarchy was observed: P-(TiVCrMo)C3 > O-Ti3C2Tx > O-(TiVCrMo)C3 > P-Ti3C2Tx. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that P-Ti3C2 strongly adsorbs O2, indicating higher surface reactivity, while oxygen termination stabilizes the surface by partially passivating Ti sites. In contrast, P-(TiVCrMo)C3 exhibits strong adsorption of oxygen-containing species due to its multi-metal electronic structure, promoting the formation of protective oxides. These results highlight the delicate balance of surface chemistry, electronic structure, and compositional entropy in designing next-generation MXene-based anticorrosion coatings for marine and industrial environments.
summary: This study demonstrates that the effectiveness of MXene-based anti-corrosion coatings depends heavily on whether the MXene is a simple binary structure or a complex medium-entropy alloy. While oxygenation helps protect against corrosion in standard Ti3C2Tx, it actually degrades the performance of more complex multi-metal MXenes by interfering with their ability to form protective oxide layers.
main_problem: Investigating how surface functionalization (oxygenation) and compositional complexity (binary vs. medium-entropy) affect the corrosion protection of MXene-reinforced epoxy coatings.
main_result: Oxygenation improves corrosion resistance in binary Ti3C2Tx MXenes but impairs it in medium-entropy (TiVCrMo)C3 MXenes; the latter performs best in its pristine state due to its ability to form protective Cr/Mo-rich oxides.
method: Experimental techniques include EIS, SEM-EDS, AFM, XPS, and XRD, complemented by DFT calculations and Electron Localization Function (ELF) analysis.
model_or_system: A nacre-inspired multilayer epoxy coating reinforced with either binary (Ti3C2Tx) or medium-entropy (TiVCrMo)C3 MXene layers, applied to C1010 carbon steel substrates.
key_observables: Impedance modulus (|Z|), coating resistance (R_coat), charge transfer resistance (R_ct), interlayer spacing, and adsorption energies of O2 and H2O.
important_parameters: Surface terminations (P- vs O-), number of coating layers (3L vs 5L), and elemental composition (binary vs medium-entropy).
figures_summary: Figures likely show SEM cross-sections of stratified layers, EDS elemental maps, XRD peak shifts for interlayer spacing, XPS spectra for chemical states, and EIS Bode/Nyquist plots showing impedance changes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of steel corrosion, describes the fabrication of multilayer MXene-epoxy coatings, presents characterization of morphology and chemistry, compares electrochemical performance of different MXene variants, uses DFT to explain the mechanistic differences in oxygen adsorption, and concludes with design principles.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of multilayer coating, illustrating hierarchical organization from macroscopic layer assembly to microscopic interfacial interactions and atomic-scale surface functionalization.

Figure 2: SEM micrographs and corresponding EDS analyses of fabricated multilayer coatings: (a) cross-sectional view showing the five-layer architecture of P-Ti3C2Tx; (b–e) corresponding EDS elemental maps; (f) top-view morphology of the P-Ti3C2Tx layer with (g–j) elemental distribution; (k) cross-sectional SEM image of the P-(TiVCrMo)C3 coating and (l–q) corresponding EDS maps; (r) top-view morphology of the P-(TiVCrMo)C3 layer with (s–x) EDS mapping.

Figure 3: AFM characterization of the MXene–epoxy interface: (a,c) surface topography image showing the step between the epoxy substrate and the deposited P-Ti3C2Tx and P-(TiVCrMo)C3 layer respectively, and (b,d) corresponding height profile. XRD plots (e) for binary MXene and (f) for medium-entropy MXene. XPS Analysis (g) high resolution spectra of Ti 2p (h) O 1s of binary MXene and (i) high resolution spectra of Cr 2p (j) high resolution spectra of O 1s of medium-entropy MXene.

Figure 4: Nyquist and Bode plots of various MXene–epoxy coatings P-Ti3C2Tx, and O-Ti3C2TX, P-(TiVCrMo)C3, and O-(TiVCrMo)C3 recorded in 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution under different conditions: (a–c) 1 h immersion, five-layer coatings; (d–f) 1 h immersion, three-layer coatings; (g–i) 24 h immersion, five-layer coatings; (j–l) 24 h immersion, three-layer coatings.
